cards.front: What three purposes do survey reports serve?
cards.back: Evidence of quality, reference for future operations on the same assets, and contractual deliverables.

cards.front: What is the key principle of survey report writing?
cards.back: Write the report as if you will never be available to explain it. In two years, you probably won't be.

cards.front: What are the five main report types in offshore survey?
cards.back: Mobilisation Report, Calibration Report, Daily Progress Report (DPR), End-of-Project Report, and As-Built Report.

cards.front: What is the typical length of a Mobilisation Report?
cards.back: 15-30 pages.

cards.front: What is the typical length of a Calibration Report?
cards.back: 5-15 pages per calibration.

cards.front: What is the typical length of a Daily Progress Report?
cards.back: 2-5 pages.

cards.front: What is the typical length of an End-of-Project Report?
cards.back: 30-80 pages.

cards.front: What is the typical length of an As-Built Report?
cards.back: 20-50 pages.

cards.front: When is a Mobilisation Report written?
cards.back: At the end of mobilisation, before survey starts.

cards.front: When is a Calibration Report written?
cards.back: After each calibration session.

cards.front: When is a Daily Progress Report written?
cards.back: At the end of each operational day.

cards.front: When is an End-of-Project Report written?
cards.back: At the end of survey operations.

cards.front: When is an As-Built Report written?
cards.back: After a construction survey.

cards.front: Who is the typical audience for a Mobilisation Report?
cards.back: Client and project manager.

cards.front: Who is the typical audience for a Calibration Report?
cards.back: Client and QA team.

cards.front: Who is the typical audience for an End-of-Project Report?
cards.back: Client and asset owner.

cards.front: Who is the typical audience for an As-Built Report?
cards.back: Client and engineering.

cards.front: What should the Mob Report cover page include?
cards.back: Project name, vessel name, client, contractor, report number, date, author, and reviewer/approver.

cards.front: What goes in the Revision History section of a mob report?
cards.back: Version number, date, author, and description of changes.

cards.front: What does the Introduction section of a mob report contain?
cards.back: Project overview, scope of survey, and reference to the contract or scope of work.

cards.front: What vessel and equipment details belong in a mob report?
cards.back: Vessel details (name, flag, dimensions), equipment list with serial numbers and cal certificate references, and equipment layout diagram or photo.

cards.front: What does the Dimensional Control Survey section include?
cards.back: Method description, results table (sensor positions in vessel reference frame), diagram showing sensor positions, and accuracy assessment.

cards.front: What system configuration details are documented in a mob report?
cards.back: Geodetic parameters (datum, projection, geoid, vertical reference), nav software config, data flow diagram, update rates, telegram formats, and time synchronisation method.

cards.front: What calibration results are summarised in the mob report?
cards.back: GNSS intersystem check, AHRS calibration, USBL calibration, MBES patch test, INS/DVL calibration (if applicable), and ancillary sensor acceptance tests.

cards.front: What does the Acceptance Testing section of a mob report cover?
cards.back: End-to-end position test results and data quality verification.

cards.front: When should the mob report structure be drafted?
cards.back: Before the mobilisation starts. Fill in sections as each phase completes to avoid last-minute rush.

cards.front: When should the mob report be substantially complete?
cards.back: Before the first survey line.

cards.front: What is the purpose of a calibration report?
cards.back: To contain enough information for an independent reviewer to assess whether the calibration was valid and the results are acceptable.

cards.front: What environmental conditions should be recorded in a calibration report?
cards.back: Sea state, wind, visibility, water depth, and current (where relevant).

cards.front: What equipment information is needed in a calibration report?
cards.back: Sensor under test (model, serial, firmware), reference sensors, and SVP data.

cards.front: What configuration details belong in a calibration report?
cards.back: Settings used such as frequency, power, range, and pulse length as applicable.

cards.front: What results must a calibration report present?
cards.back: Determined offsets/corrections, residuals (SD, RMS, max error), comparison with previous cal values (if available), and pass/fail assessment against acceptance criteria.

cards.front: What plots should be included in a calibration report?
cards.back: Time series of residuals, before/after comparison, scatter plots (e.g., USBL dX/dY), and coverage plot with line plan.

cards.front: What is the MBES Patch Test acceptance criterion for roll, pitch, and heading offsets?
cards.back: Less than 1.0 degree each, per IHO S-44 and project spec.

cards.front: What is the MBES Patch Test acceptance criterion for latency?
cards.back: 0-100 ms, consistent, per project spec.

cards.front: What is the USBL calibration acceptance criterion for SD of residuals?
cards.back: Less than 0.5 m at operational depth, per IMCA S 017.

cards.front: What is the INS/DVL calibration acceptance criterion for scale factor?
cards.back: 0.95-1.05, per manufacturer spec.

cards.front: What is the AHRS acceptance criterion for heading C-O?
cards.back: Less than 0.5 degrees, per IMCA S 017.

cards.front: What is the Static GNSS acceptance criterion for horizontal agreement?
cards.back: Less than 1.0 m (DGNSS), per project spec.

cards.front: What approval signatures are needed on a calibration report?
cards.back: Calibrated by, reviewed by, and approved by (all with dates).

cards.front: What does DPR stand for?
cards.back: Daily Progress Report.

cards.front: What is the DPR?
cards.back: The project's daily diary that records what was achieved, what went wrong, and what is planned for the next period.

cards.front: What goes in the DPR header?
cards.back: Project name, vessel name, date, report number, and DPR period (e.g., 0600-0600 UTC).

cards.front: What operational statistics are tracked in a DPR?
cards.back: Hours operational/standby/weather downtime/equipment downtime/transit, survey lines completed vs planned, coverage achieved (% of area or route), and data volume acquired.

cards.front: What vessel information is recorded in each DPR?
cards.back: Vessel position at report time: latitude, longitude, and heading.

cards.front: What is the DPR best practice for recording events?
cards.back: Write the DPR as events happen, not from memory at the end of the shift. Keep a running log during the day and include times for all events.

cards.front: Why is accurate DPR timekeeping so important?
cards.back: The DPR is often the primary record used to resolve disputes about operational time and downtime.

cards.front: What does EOP stand for in survey reporting?
cards.back: End-of-Project.

cards.front: What is the EOP report?
cards.back: The final deliverable that summarises the entire survey campaign.

cards.front: What should the Executive Summary of an EOP report contain?
cards.back: A 1-page overview of the project, key achievements, and any significant issues.

cards.front: What survey parameters are documented in the EOP report?
cards.back: Geodetic parameters, vertical reference, accuracy requirements and how they were met.

cards.front: What does the Survey Operations section of the EOP report include?
cards.back: Chronological narrative of operations, coverage achieved (with charts/maps), and any deviations from planned scope with justification.

cards.front: What data processing information belongs in the EOP report?
cards.back: Processing workflow, software used, QC procedures applied, and outlier/rejection criteria.

cards.front: What results are presented in the EOP report?
cards.back: Summary of key findings, data quality assessment (achieved vs required accuracy), and any anomalies or features of note.

cards.front: What is the Deliverables List in an EOP report?
cards.back: An itemised list of all deliverables with format, file naming, and delivery method.

cards.front: What are the rules for presenting numerical data in tables?
cards.back: Use consistent units (stated in column headers), right-align numerical values, include column headers on every page, and round to appropriate precision.

cards.front: What key requirements apply to a track chart?
cards.back: North arrow, scale bar, coordinate grid, and legend.

cards.front: What key requirements apply to a time series plot?
cards.back: Axis labels, units, time format (UTC), and event markers.

cards.front: What key requirement applies to scatter plots showing position data?
cards.back: Equal aspect ratio for position data and a circle showing the tolerance.

cards.front: What must be stated on cross-section plots?
cards.back: Vertical exaggeration and depth convention (positive down or up).

cards.front: Why is stating vertical exaggeration critical?
cards.back: A 10x VE makes a 1 degree slope look like 10 degrees, which can mislead clients and engineers.

cards.front: What coordinate presentation rules should be followed?
cards.back: State datum and projection on every chart and table, use consistent format throughout, state coordinate order explicitly, and include zone number for UTM.

cards.front: What are the two acceptable coordinate formats mentioned?
cards.back: DD.DDDDDD (decimal degrees) or DD MM SS.SSS (degrees minutes seconds). Pick one and be consistent.

cards.front: Where is calibration validity documented as a QC check?
cards.back: In the Mob Report, at the start of the project.

cards.front: Where is SVP currency documented as a QC check?
cards.back: In the DPR and/or processing notes, for each cast.

cards.front: Where is position system status documented?
cards.back: In the DPR, daily.

cards.front: Where is cross-line comparison documented?
cards.back: In the processing report, per survey area.

cards.front: Where is tide verification documented?
cards.back: In the processing notes, daily.

cards.front: Where is data completeness documented?
cards.back: In coverage charts, at the end of each area.

cards.front: Where is artefact assessment documented?
cards.back: In the processing report, per deliverable.

cards.front: What is the standard document numbering format?
cards.back: PROJECTCODE-VESSEL-DOCTYPE-SEQUENCE. Example: PRJ001-VESSEL01-MOB-001.

cards.front: What does Rev 0 (initial) mean in the revision control scheme?
cards.back: Draft -- initial issue for internal review.

cards.front: What does Rev A mean in the revision control scheme?
cards.back: Issued for Review (IFR) -- issued to client for comment.

cards.front: What does Rev B mean in the revision control scheme?
cards.back: Issued for Review -- incorporating client comments.

cards.front: What does Rev 0 (final) mean in the revision control scheme?
cards.back: Issued for Use (IFU) -- final approved version.

cards.front: What is IFR in document revision terminology?
cards.back: Issued for Review -- the document is sent to the client for comment.

cards.front: What is IFU in document revision terminology?
cards.back: Issued for Use -- the final approved version of the document.

cards.front: How should client comments on a report be handled?
cards.back: Log each comment with your response and action taken, then issue the next revision with a comment response sheet for a clear audit trail.

cards.front: List 5 common causes for a report being rejected by a client.
cards.back: 1) Missing sections or incomplete content, 2) Acceptance criteria not stated or not referenced, 3) Cal results without pass/fail assessment, 4) Charts without north arrows/scale bars/coordinates, 5) Inconsistent units or coordinate formats.

cards.front: How do you prevent data mismatches between reports?
cards.back: Use a single source of truth (processed data, not handwritten notes), cross-check key values between DPR and EOP, and explicitly state and justify any values that changed.

cards.front: What two IMCA guidelines are referenced for survey reporting?
cards.back: IMCA S 015 (Guidelines for Survey Mobilisation and Calibration) and IMCA D 045 (Guidelines for Survey Report Content).

cards.front: What goes in the Appendices of a mob report?
cards.back: Calibration certificates, detailed calibration results, and equipment photographs.

cards.front: What information does the data flow diagram in a mob report convey?
cards.back: What equipment connects to what -- showing the data flow between sensors and processing systems.

cards.front: What should you do if a calibration value is refined between mob and EOP?
cards.back: State the change explicitly in the EOP report with justification for why the value was refined.
